name: business_strategist
output:
## Diagnosis

You don't have a product problem — you have a customer problem. Building tools before getting paid clients is backwards; service businesses validate demand by selling first, then building/delivering. Your prices are all wrong for an unknown operator in a mid-size market like Sioux Falls — $29 SEO audits and $12 blog posts signal cheapness, not value, and require you to close 30+ clients monthly just to pay bills. The real issue isn't your stack (Hermes, Russell Tuna, heartbeat, etc.) — it's that you've built a factory with no salespeople and no leads walking in the door.

---

## 3 Specific Actions

**1. Pick ONE service, raise the price to $497–$997, and sell it to 5 people this week.**
Don't build anything new. Your "SEO audit" at $29 is dead on arrival — pivot it to a done-for-you SEO strategy session + audit at $497. Frame it as: "I spent 2 hours in your website doing a full SEO diagnostic + a written action plan. If you implement it and don't see traffic improvement in 60 days, I'll refund you." Sell this to 5 businesses YOU ALREADY KNOW (HVAC companies, dentists, real estate agents in Sioux Falls). Not strangers. People who've seen you work.

**2. Use Russell Tuna to send 10 personalized messages TODAY.**
Not broadcast spam. Real messages: "Hey [Name], I just finished a project doing SEO for an HVAC company — took their site from page 3 to the top of Google Maps in 6 weeks. You want me to take a quick look at your site and tell you what I'd do? No cost, just feedback." Target local businesses. This is your entire sales pipeline for Week 1.

**3. Document ONE case study from your first 3 clients, no matter how small.**
Before/after screenshots, what the client said, what you delivered. This is your entire marketing engine right now. A single documented result is worth more than your entire tool stack. Put it on a simple page at `josephv.zo.space` — "Results." That's your homepage now.

---

## What to AVOID

- **Don't build another tool, feature, or integration.** Every hour you spend on Hermes, SkillClaw, or new automation is an hour you're not selling. Your stack is already overkill for where you are.
- **Don't launch on Upwork, Fiverr, or any freelance marketplace.** The race to the bottom will eat you alive at these price points, and you'll spend months grinding for $12 blog posts with zero repeatability.

---

## Confidence Score: **6/10**

Here's the honest reasoning: Joseph CAN build, which is genuinely rare and valuable. The tools exist. The Telegram bot is a real asset for client communication. But the behavioral pattern — spreading thin, never finishing completely — is the actual risk. A strategy is only as good as its execution, and history suggests he'll start 3 things instead of finishing 1. The plan above is designed to be impossible to spread thin on — it's literally 3 steps and then wait for money.

---

## #1 Risk: He Gets Bored of Selling and Goes Back to Building

The moment he doesn't close clients in Week 1 (which is likely, because selling is uncomfortable), he'll convince himself the tools need one more feature. Then he'll spend 2 weeks rebuilding something that was already finished. **The failure mode isn't bad strategy — it's strategy abandonment.** Lock in the 3 actions above and do nothing else until at least one person pays you real money.

name: technical_architect
output:
## Honest Diagnosis

Joseph has a live homepage for his best HVAC lead but **no working product, no delivery pipeline, and no Stripe-connected service to sell**. His stack is scattered across 6 forked repos, 94 Hermes skills that "often go down," a CashClaw CLI that doesn't even run, and half a dozen Zo Space pages that are disconnected from any revenue flow. The homepage for EZ Heating & Cooling is the closest thing he has to a funnel — but it's a static page with a phone number, not a system that can receive a client, deliver work, and collect money autonomously. The problem isn't tools. The problem is that every tool he has is **half-integrated and none of them are wired together into a single path from prospect → delivery → payment**.

---

## 3 Specific Actions

**1. Define one offer, one price, one Stripe link — today.**
Pick the simplest service Joseph can actually deliver: run `cashclaw audit --url [site] --tier standard` using Groq API right now. Set price at $99 (EZ Heating won't flinch at $99 if the audit shows $5K+ in SEO opportunities). Create a Stripe payment link at `buy.stripe.com` for $99. The homepage already has the contact form — wire it to that payment link. That's the entire funnel.

**2. Run the SEO audit for EZ Heating & Cooling before calling it a lead.**
Use Groq API (free tier) or OpenRouter credits to run the audit. CashClaw CLI doesn't need to be installed — use `curl` to call Groq directly with a structured SEO audit prompt. Show Jon the actual findings: what keywords he's missing, what his competitors rank for, what his Google Business Profile gaps are. This is the meeting opener that earns the conversation.

**3. Wire one Telegram bot to send the audit report.**
Use Russell Tuna or the existing Telegram connection to send the completed audit PDF to Jon via text message with the Stripe payment link. One message, one link, one action for Jon to take. No forms, no follow-up sequences, no CRM. Just: "Here's your free audit. Here's how to pay $99 if you want the full action plan."

---

## What to AVOID

**Don't touch Hermes, n8n, or any "orchestration layer" right now.** Hermes is unreliable and n8n requires a local machine setup Joseph doesn't have running. Any time spent wiring multi-agent pipelines is time not spent closing a client. The shortest path to $100 is: Groq → SEO audit → Stripe link → Telegram message.

**Don't build more Zo Space pages.** Joseph already has 120+ routes on his zo.space site. Every new page is a shiny object. The only page that matters right now is the one that converts EZ Heating & Cooling — and that page already exists at `josephv.zo.space`.

---

## Confidence Score: **4/10**

**Why:** The minimum viable path exists — Groq API is free, Stripe is connected, Telegram is wired, the homepage is live. Joseph could theoretically close this client with zero new tools. But the execution gaps are real: CashClaw doesn't run, the SEO audit workflow isn't automated, and nobody has actually messaged Jon Zokaites yet. The stack is "almost there" but the last 10% (the actual human outreach and payment link delivery) hasn't happened. Without that, confidence stays low.

---

## #1 Risk: **Jon Zokaites never checks his phone/email**

The hardest part isn't the technical stack — it's that this is a solo HVAC contractor in Sioux Falls who's probably not sitting around waiting for an AI audit from a stranger. Joseph needs a reason for Jon to **open the email, care about the findings, and click the Stripe link**. If Joseph just sends a generic "I ran an SEO audit on your site" pitch, it goes nowhere. The audit has to show something Jon can't ignore — a competitor winning his customers, or a keyword gap costing him real revenue. If Joseph can't frame the audit findings in terms Jon cares about (jobs booked, revenue lost), no technical stack will save the deal.

name: marketer
output:
## Honest Diagnosis

You've built a workshop full of tools but have no customers knocking on the door. The good news: you already have a warm lead sitting in front of you — EZ Heating & Cooling is mentioned on YOUR OWN website, which means you have at minimum an existing relationship or shared professional network. You don't need more products, more tools, or more automation. You need one sale, one proof point, and one referral. That's it.

---

## 3 Specific Actions

**1. Call EZ Heating & Cooling today. Not email. Not Telegram. Call.**
Say: *"Hey, I'm Joseph. I noticed we both do HVAC work in Sioux Falls — I help companies like yours get more calls using AI. I'd love to show you something I built specifically for HVAC contractors. Can I send you a quick 2-minute video or meet for 15 minutes this week?"* Your goal is a discovery call or a small trial, not a big pitch. If they say yes, give them your $29 SEO audit or $15 lead gen tool for free — just to get a result you can screenshot.

**2. Turn that result into one screenshot, one quote, one case study.**
It doesn't need to be fancy. A before/after of their Google ranking, a screenshot of leads generated, anything. Then go back to them and say: *"Hey, we got these results in 48 hours. If you want to scale this, my full service is $X/month."* You now have social proof AND a warm client who already trusts you.

**3. Use Russell Tuna to send 20 HVAC contractors in Sioux Falls a voice message or text.**
Not a spam blast. A real message: *"Hey, I just helped a local HVAC company in Sioux Falls get [X result]. I think we could do the same for you. You interested in a quick 10-minute call?"* 20 messages, 2-3 responses, 1 sale. That's the math that works.

---

## What to AVOID

- **Don't build another tool, page, or automation.** You have everything you need already. Adding more inventory before you have revenue is rearranging deck chairs.
- **Don't try to cold email a full funnel.** You have zero proof. Cold outreach without social proof gets ignored or marked spam. Your Telegram list and warm network is where you start.

---

## Confidence Score: 8/10

Because: You have one warm prospect already in your orbit, you have real tools that work, and the market (HVAC contractors) pays $500-2K/month for lead generation services. The path from where you are to $1K is shorter than you think — it's one conversation, not one more product.

---

## #1 Risk

**You don't ask.** You build another thing instead of picking up the phone or sending the message. The tools are ready. The only variable is whether you actually reach out to a human being and offer them something. If you wait for perfect, you'll never get there.

name: hvac_industry_expert
output:
**1. Honest Diagnosis**

HVAC contractors in Sioux Falls are mostly 2–8 truck shops running on gut instinct, not spreadsheets. They've been burned by "SEO guys" and "lead gen" pitches that promised the world and delivered nothing. They don't fear AI — they don't understand it and assume it'll be another bill with no proof it helped. The only language they speak is **"I got a new customer call today that paid $400."** Right now, $200-500/month is real money to them — that's a week of revenue or more — so the barrier to entry isn't just skepticism, it's fear of being robbed. Joseph's product is actually well-positioned IF he stops selling features and starts selling meals.

---

**2. Three Specific Actions**

1. **Lead with a live pilot, not a pitch deck.** Do free SEO audits for 3 shops (they're quick, he can knock them out in an hour) and personally walk them through the results — not on Zoom, in their shop while they're between jobs. Show them one specific keyword they're missing that their zip code neighbors rank for. The goal isn't the audit — it's getting a foot in the door so when you say "I can get you 5 new leads this month," they already trust you enough to say yes. Free work up front = credibility deposit.

2. **Rephrase everything around "jobs, not leads."** HVAC guys hear "leads" and think "spam form submissions." Change the language entirely:
   - Don't say: "I'll improve your local SEO"
   - Say: "I'll get your number showing up when someone in Sioux Falls types 'furnace repair near me' — real phone calls, not website visitors"
   - Don't say: "$299/month for AI-powered outreach"
   - Say: "$299/month and if you don't get 5 new jobs from my work, I don't get paid"
   - **A money-back guarantee tied to jobs is the only thing that breaks their skepticism.**

3. **Target the right 10 shops and call them by name.** Sioux Falls HVAC market is small enough that one person can realistically own the relationship with 20-30 shops. Pick the 10 most active (check their Google Maps listing age, reviews, how fast they respond) and:
   - Mail a one-page letter (yes, physical mail — it stands out) with a simple value prop and his cell number
   - Follow up with a 90-second cold call: "Hey this is Joseph, I'm working with a couple shops in Sioux Falls on their new customer calls. You busy right now? [if yes] Cool, I'll send you a quick text with how I help shops get 5 more calls a month — if it doesn't make sense, just delete it."
   - **No pressure, no meeting request, just a conversation starter.**

---

**3. What to AVOID**

- **Don't lead with SEO audits, blog content, or "competitor analysis" as products.** These mean nothing to a guy who just wants his phone to ring. If he asks what you do, the answer is: "I get you more new customer calls from Google and I only charge you if it works." The services you listed are *how* you deliver, not *what* you're selling.
- **Don't cold email.** HVAC contractors don't read cold emails — they delete them without reading. Email feels impersonal and feels like every other vendor who wants their credit card. One-on-one phone calls or in-person visits at their shop are the only real in.

---

**4. Confidence: 7/10**

The reason it's not higher: the hardest part isn't the strategy — it's execution consistency. Most people who try this quit after 10–15 "no's" without realizing that in a town like Sioux Falls, a "no" today can become a "yes" in six months when the guy's current lead gen service disappoints him. The plan works if he follows up. The risk is that Joseph treats this like a spray-and-pray campaign instead of a relationship business. If he commits to 90 days of consistent outreach to the same 20 shops, I think he lands 3–5 paying clients. If he gives up after two weeks, this falls apart.

---

**5. #1 Risk That Could Make This Fail**

**He positions himself as another "marketing guy" before proving a single result.** If he hands out business cards, sends emails, or makes pitches that sound like every other digital marketing vendor that's burned these contractors, he'll get lumped into the same bucket and permanently closed off. The only way this works is if the *first* job he does for a contractor produces undeniable results — and then he uses that real result as his entire sales pitch. "I did X for Smith's HVAC and they got 7 new calls last month. Want to see if I can do the same for you?" That's the unlock. Without a proven case study in his own market, everything else is uphill.
